feedback.text: Videogames used to be products purchased that would still work decades later so long as the console it originally released on was still functional. This included lending physical copies of videogames to friends, something which Corporations in the gaming sphere have not only accepted but even encouraged as word of mouth marketing. This idea is now actively under attack by the very corporations that used to put so much faith and value on player-reception for the sole reason of fattening shareholder's bottom line. What used to be products are now being turned into services and, per EULA/Terms of Service, the idea of lending one's physical copy to someone is now against the rules, as it now doesn't involve simply passing a digital disk to someone else, but giving access to an account via its password, which, by and large, is considered against the ToS by major companies. The slippery slope here is hard to miss. If any product can be turned (some might say demoted) into a service, quality assurances no longer apply. Consumer rights are thrown out the window. Consumer protection laws become an afterthought. It stands to reason that allowing this, as is the case with the current state of videogames, sets not just a bad precedent, but a dangerous one. If any product can be turned into a subscription service, who is to say that consumers will have any control left over the contents of their own homes by the end of the 20s? You wouldn't subscribe for a chair. You wouldnt subscribe for your car's seat heater. You shouldn't treat an 80 product like it's a subscription service that just happens to retroactively be a cost of X per however many months the publisher supports access to the game. This is untenable in any context outside the digital world, and treating this any different than real products would be very digitally unfair indeed.
